input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin delivers a portfolio of services: (1) real‑time monitoring of system states (e.g., temperature, humidity, equipment status); (2) predictive analytics and forecasting of energy usage and equipment performance; (3) simulation of what‑if scenarios to evaluate design or operational changes; (4) life‑cycle encompassing optimization that continuously reduces operational costs and improves energy efficiency; (5) remote control functionality that allows the Facility Manager to operate physical infrastructure (e.g., adjust HVAC set‑points, switch lighting) through the Twin; and (6) interactive 3D visualization of the building using a game‑engine environment. The primary users are Facility Managers and other building stakeholders who require insight, decision support, and direct interaction with the physical asset.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The DT operates with live, continuous synchronization between the physical building and its digital counterpart, using real‑time monitoring of sensor data to keep the virtual model up‑to‑date. Synchronisation is performed continuously (i.e., as soon as new data become available), providing a real‑time view of the asset. No explicit latency bounds or discrete time‑step intervals are specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system follows a modular hub‑and‑spoke architecture where a central DT framework acts as the coordination hub. It integrates BIM models, the Eclipse Hono IoT messaging backbone, the Eclipse Ditto digital‑twin engine, and a 3‑D visualisation layer (Unreal Engine) via defined APIs and message brokers. Extension points allow plug‑ins, and the framework orchestrates data flow and resource management across these components.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is realised through the incorporation of AECO‑specific standards such as BIM (e.g., building information models) and by coupling the DT to building control systems via Eclipse Hono and Eclipse Ditto. These components enable bidirectional data exchange between the physical building control infrastructure and the digital twin, allowing sensor data to flow into the twin and control signals to be sent back to the building systems. The architecture therefore supports interoperability with existing enterprise AECO tools and control platforms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The framework enriches Building Information Modeling (BIM) with explicit device definitions—sensors, actuators, and controllers—creating a unified semantic foundation that bridges static geometric BIM data with dynamic behavioral information. Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) techniques are applied to extract structured representations from these enriched BIM models and automatically generate deployable Digital Twin (DT) artifacts, minimizing manual coding. Real‑time data exchange continuously synchronises physical sensor/actuator streams with the DT, while interactive visualization layers map the data onto the 3‑D BIM geometry. The modular architecture exposes standardized extension points allowing custom analytics and services to consume the generated models and data.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation logic is modelled directly inside the BIM environment as controller entities linked to physical actuators (e.g., HVAC valves, motorized blinds). Building Control Engineers specify control capabilities and remote‑control interfaces within the BIM model, which are then materialised as executable DT services via the MDE pipeline. Real‑time data exchange mechanisms propagate command signals to the physical devices, enabling Facility Managers to operate the infrastructure through the DT interface. The framework’s modular extension points permit integration of additional actuator drivers or safety‑constraint checks as custom plugins.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors (e.g., temperature, humidity, occupancy) are defined as first‑class objects in the enriched BIM model, each associated with metadata describing measurement purpose and logical location. The DT framework continuously streams sensor readings through its real‑time data exchange layer, providing Facility Managers with up‑to‑date state information and supporting interactive visualisation of the sensed values on the BIM geometry. Custom service extensions can ingest these streams for analytics or higher‑level decision making.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Before DT generation the input BIM model is validated, and the resulting twin’s fidelity is quantified using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric, which measures how closely the digital representation reflects the physical counterpart. The framework thus incorporates model validation, metric‑based fidelity assessment, and MDE‑driven transformation to ensure data and model quality throughout the deployment lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The framework provides real‑time data exchange that continuously synchronises sensor and actuator data from the physical building to its digital twin, ensuring up‑to‑date virtual representation; the document does not detail specific data types, communication protocols, transmission frequencies, event triggers, quality‑assurance methods, preprocessing steps, or integration mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Bidirectional communication is supported, allowing the digital twin to transmit control signals back to the building management system for real‑time monitoring and control, but the document gives no specifics on command formats, validation, authentication, feedback, conflict resolution, or execution confirmation procedures.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: A modular architecture with standardized extension points and non‑functional requirements for interoperability, scalability, and maintainability is described, yet the document does not specify the underlying network protocols, topology, bandwidth, latency, redundancy, or security measures used for the technical connection.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The digital twin framework is presented as a centrally managed, runtime‑configurable hub that can integrate custom services and analytics, indicating flexibility in deployment; however, the document does not provide concrete information on hosting platforms, compute/storage resources, scalability mechanisms, deployment models, or availability/reliability assurances.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The framework supports a complete Digital Twin life‑cycle that starts with device modeling inside a BIM environment where Building Control Engineers define sensors, actuators and controllers and embed control logic. The enriched BIM model constitutes the design‑time semantic representation. Using Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) principles the model is automatically transformed into a structured intermediate representation and then into deployable DT artifacts (runtime services, data adapters, visualisation components). These artifacts are instantiated on the execution platform, establishing the operational representation that continuously synchronises with the physical building via real‑time data exchange. Throughout the cycle, transition between phases is managed automatically by the model‑driven transformation pipeline, while governance is enforced by the defined actor roles (Building Control Engineer, Facility Manager, DT Engineer) and by the modular architecture with standardized extension points that control how custom services are integrated and evolved.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process follows a model‑driven methodology: stakeholders first model devices and control logic in BIM, then the DT Engineer applies MDE‑based extraction tools to generate a formal model that is automatically compiled into deployable twin components. The modular architecture provides standardized extension points where custom services and analytics plugins can be added, allowing the twin to evolve as operational requirements change. Real‑time data exchange mechanisms keep the twin and the physical asset synchronized, and interactive visualisation interfaces enable monitoring and control. While explicit quality‑assurance or version‑control practices are not detailed, the process relies on automated generation and deployment to minimise manual errors and to support iterative evolution of the twin.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The DT offers decision‑support through continuous real‑time data exchange that keeps the digital replica synchronized with the physical building, and through interactive visualization (e.g., immersive interfaces) that enable Facility Managers to monitor system states and issue control actions. The modular architecture permits the integration of custom analytics services, allowing the twin to generate real‑time insights, predictive analytics, and recommendations for building operation. Users interact via the visualization dashboards and control interfaces; however, specific confidence metrics or recommendation validation mechanisms are not described in the document.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The solution adopts standardization at several levels. It builds on BIM as an industry‑wide standard modeling base and enriches BIM semantics to represent sensors, actuators and controllers, thereby standardizing the data model. The framework’s modular design provides 'standardized extension points' that define uniform interfaces for adding custom services or analytics, ensuring interoperability between components. While no external standards (e.g., ISO, IFC) are explicitly cited, the approach emphasizes a standardized methodology for model transformation and artifact generation, positioning the framework as a standard solution for DT deployment in the construction sector.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly names specific tools and enablers used in the framework, including Eclipse Hono, Ditto, Unreal Engine, and Building Information Modeling (BIM).

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly describes the DT's live synchronization and real-time monitoring but notes the absence of explicit latency bounds. The sources support the real-time aspects but do not specify technical details like latency or time-step intervals. Quotes: 'Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins (R3)', 'Our approach integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange'.

The source documents focus on a model-driven framework for deploying a Digital Twin for infrastructure and mention 'twinnable devices' within that context. They do not describe multiple Digital Twin instances, hierarchical structures between twins, or coordination mechanisms between separate twin instances. Therefore, stating 'Not in Document' is correct.

The extraction accurately describes the framework's architecture, including the central hub role, modularity, and the integration of specific components like BIM, Eclipse Hono, Eclipse Ditto, and Unreal Engine. The mention of standardized extension points and orchestration is also directly supported.
Supported by quotes: 'The DT Framework acts as a central hub', 'A modular architecture with defined extension points to support scalable, service-oriented DT development', 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange and leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization'.

The extraction correctly highlights integration with AECO standards like BIM, coupling to building control systems via Eclipse Hono and Ditto, and bidirectional data exchange. The sources explicitly mention these integrations and their purposes. Quotes: 'Our approach integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry', 'DTs require continuous synchronization with physical assets through sensor data, the ability to process and analyze this data in real-time, and mechanisms to feed control signals back to the BCS'.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

All these points are explicitly detailed in the source text. Like the actuating components, it lists specific sensor types ('temperature, humidity, occupancy') that are not explicitly listed in the source text (the text refers generally to 'sensors' or 'environmental conditions'). The rest of the description regarding the framework's handling of sensor data is accurate. Supported by quotes: 'define sensors... directly within the BIM environment', 'continuous synchronization', and 'interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces'.

The extraction accurately captures the validation process and the specific metric used for fidelity assessment in the paper. It correctly links the ODTE metric to the reflection of the physical counterpart and mentions the MDE transformation for quality assurance. Supported by quotes: 'after validating the model [65], the model is fed into the Hono/Ditto Configurator', 'evaluate the entanglement of the resulting DT using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric', 'measuring how well the resulting DT reflects its physical counterpart'.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
